comment_text.sn12.24:1.5: Sāriputta, like his teacher, seeks out meaningful discussion with people who are not Buddhist.
comment_text.sn12.24:3.4: They believed in the efficacy of deeds, but not that such deeds created suffering (or “pleasure and pain” in the next discourse). This seems odd, but it’s not irrational. Perhaps they believed that one’s deeds create a new life, but that the experiences of pleasure and pain in that life arise from circumstances beyond the control of oneself or others.
comment_text.sn12.24:3.6: The wanderers frame their question carefully and respectfully, so Sāriputta answers them directly and affirms their manner of questioning.
comment_text.sn12.24:4.3: The wanderers have already shown their epistemological caution, so, in identifying “contact” as the key dependency, Sāriputta indicates one of the most directly observable factors of dependent origination. But contact is also a key underlying cause of wrong views (DN 1:3.45.1).
comment_text.sn12.24:6.1: Sāriputta clarifies the meaning of “dependent”: it is something without which the other cannot be experienced, i.e. it is a necessary condition.
comment_text.sn12.24:8.5: The explicit affirmation and repetition of information reinforces the correct text in the memory and wards off creeping disinformation.
comment_text.sn12.24:17.2: Compare with attho vutto in the Itivuttaka. Here the sense is that the topic can be summed up with one statement.
comment_text.sn12.24:17.3: The phrase “deep and appears deep” is spoken twice elsewhere, also by Ānanda in the context of dependent origination (DN 15:1.5, SN 12.60:1.4).
comment_text.sn12.24:21.9: The Buddha recapitulates, confirming that the sequence here goes back only as far as the six senses. This agrees with DN 1:3.71.12, which likewise goes back to the six senses. There, the factors beginning with ignorance are implied under wrong view.

root_text.sn12.24:1.2: Atha kho āyasmā sāriputto pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya rājagahaṁ piṇḍāya pāvisi.
root_text.sn12.24:1.5: Yannūnāhaṁ yena aññatitthiyānaṁ paribbājakānaṁ ārāmo tenupasaṅkameyyan”ti.
root_text.sn12.24:2.1: Atha kho āyasmā sāriputto yena aññatitthiyānaṁ paribbājakānaṁ ārāmo tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā tehi aññatitthiyehi paribbājakehi saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn12.24:2.2: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinnaṁ kho āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ te aññatitthiyā paribbājakā etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn12.24:3.1: “Santāvuso sāriputta, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.24:3.2: Santi panāvuso sāriputta, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā paraṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.24:3.3: Santāvuso sāriputta, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.24:3.4: Santi panāvuso sāriputta, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.24:3.5: Idha, panāvuso sāriputta, samaṇo gotamo kiṁvādī kimakkhāyī?
root_text.sn12.24:3.6: Kathaṁ byākaramānā ca mayaṁ vuttavādino ceva samaṇassa gotamassa assāma, na ca samaṇaṁ gotamaṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyyāma, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyyāma, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyyā”ti?
root_text.sn12.24:4.4: Iti vadaṁ vuttavādī ceva bhagavato assa, na ca bhagavantaṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyya, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyya, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyya.
root_text.sn12.24:5.1: Tatrāvuso, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.24:5.2: Yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā paraṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.24:5.3: Yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca dukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.24:5.4: Yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.24:6.1: Tatrāvuso, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.24:6.2: Yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā paraṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.24:6.3: Yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.24:6.4: Yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjatī”ti.
root_text.sn12.24:7.1: Assosi kho āyasmā ānando āyasmato sāriputtassa tehi aññatitthiyehi paribbājakehi saddhiṁ imaṁ kathāsallāpaṁ.
root_text.sn12.24:7.2: Atha kho āyasmā ānando rājagahe piṇḍāya caritvā pacchābhattaṁ piṇḍapātapaṭikkanto yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi.
root_text.sn12.24:7.3: Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā ānando yāvatako āyasmato sāriputtassa tehi aññatitthiyehi paribbājakehi saddhiṁ ahosi kathāsallāpo taṁ sabbaṁ bhagavato ārocesi.
root_text.sn12.24:8.1: “Sādhu sādhu, ānanda, yathā taṁ sāriputto sammā byākaramāno byākareyya.
root_text.sn12.24:8.2: Paṭiccasamuppannaṁ kho, ānanda, dukkhaṁ vuttaṁ mayā.
root_text.sn12.24:8.5: Iti vadaṁ vuttavādī ceva me assa, na ca maṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyya, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyya, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyya.
root_text.sn12.24:9.1: Tatrānanda, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.24:9.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.24:10.1: Tatrānanda, yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.24:10.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.24:11.1: Ekamidāhaṁ, ānanda, samayaṁ idheva rājagahe viharāmi veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
root_text.sn12.24:11.2: Atha khvāhaṁ, ānanda, pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya rājagahaṁ piṇḍāya pāvisiṁ.
root_text.sn12.24:11.5: Yannūnāhaṁ yena aññatitthiyānaṁ paribbājakānaṁ ārāmo tenupasaṅkameyyan’ti.
root_text.sn12.24:12.1: Atha khvāhaṁ, ānanda, yena aññatitthiyānaṁ paribbājakānaṁ ārāmo tenupasaṅkamiṁ; upasaṅkamitvā tehi aññatitthiyehi paribbājakehi saddhiṁ sammodiṁ.
root_text.sn12.24:12.2: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁ. Ekamantaṁ nisinnaṁ kho maṁ, ānanda, te aññatitthiyā paribbājakā etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn12.24:13.1: ‘Santāvuso gotama, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.24:13.2: Santi panāvuso gotama, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā paraṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.24:13.3: Santāvuso gotama, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.24:13.4: Santi panāvuso gotama, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.24:13.5: Idha no āyasmā gotamo kiṁvādī kimakkhāyī?
root_text.sn12.24:13.6: Kathaṁ byākaramānā ca mayaṁ vuttavādino ceva āyasmato gotamassa assāma, na ca āyasmantaṁ gotamaṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyyāma, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyyāma, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyyā’ti?
root_text.sn12.24:14.1: Evaṁ vuttāhaṁ, ānanda, te aññatitthiye paribbājake etadavocaṁ:
root_text.sn12.24:14.5: Iti vadaṁ vuttavādī ceva me assa, na ca maṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyya, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyya, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyyāti.
root_text.sn12.24:15.1: Tatrāvuso, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.24:15.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.24:16.1: Tatrāvuso, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.24:16.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjatī’”ti.
root_text.sn12.24:17.2: Yatra hi nāma ekena padena sabbo attho vutto bhavissati.
root_text.sn12.24:17.3: Siyā nu kho, bhante, esevattho vitthārena vuccamāno gambhīro ceva assa gambhīrāvabhāso cā”ti?
root_text.sn12.24:18.3: ‘jarāmaraṇaṁ, āvuso ānanda, kiṁnidānaṁ kiṁsamudayaṁ kiṁjātikaṁ kiṁpabhavan’ti?
root_text.sn12.24:18.5: ‘jarāmaraṇaṁ kho, āvuso, jātinidānaṁ jātisamudayaṁ jātijātikaṁ jātipabhavan’ti.
root_text.sn12.24:19.2: ‘jāti panāvuso ānanda, kiṁnidānā kiṁsamudayā kiṁjātikā kiṁpabhavā’ti?
root_text.sn12.24:19.4: ‘jāti kho, āvuso, bhavanidānā bhavasamudayā bhavajātikā bhavappabhavā’ti.
root_text.sn12.24:20.2: ‘bhavo panāvuso ānanda, kiṁnidāno kiṁsamudayo kiṁjātiko kiṁpabhavo’ti?
root_text.sn12.24:20.4: ‘bhavo kho, āvuso, upādānanidāno upādānasamudayo upādānajātiko upādānappabhavo’ti.
root_text.sn12.24:21.6: ‘phasso panāvuso ānanda, kiṁnidāno kiṁsamudayo kiṁjātiko kiṁpabhavo’ti?
root_text.sn12.24:21.8: ‘phasso kho, āvuso, saḷāyatananidāno saḷāyatanasamudayo saḷāyatanajātiko saḷāyatanappabhavo’ti.

translation_text.sn12.24:1.1: Near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove.
translation_text.sn12.24:1.2: Then Venerable Sāriputta robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Rājagaha for alms.
translation_text.sn12.24:1.4: “It’s too early to wander for alms in Rājagaha.
translation_text.sn12.24:1.5: Why don’t I visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?”
translation_text.sn12.24:2.1: Then he went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there.
translation_text.sn12.24:2.2: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. The wanderers said to him:
translation_text.sn12.24:3.1: “Reverend Sāriputta, there are ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. Some of them declare that suffering is made by oneself.
translation_text.sn12.24:3.2: Some of them declare that suffering is made by another.
translation_text.sn12.24:3.3: Some of them declare that suffering is made by both oneself and another.
translation_text.sn12.24:3.4: Some of them declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
translation_text.sn12.24:3.5: What does the ascetic Gotama say about this? How does he explain it?
translation_text.sn12.24:3.6: How should we answer so as to repeat what the ascetic Gotama has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? How should we explain in line with his teaching, with no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?”
translation_text.sn12.24:4.1: “Reverends, the Buddha has said that suffering is dependently originated.
translation_text.sn12.24:4.4: In speaking like this, you would repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth. You would explain in line with his teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.
translation_text.sn12.24:5.1: Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact.
translation_text.sn12.24:5.2: In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by another, that’s also dependent on contact.
translation_text.sn12.24:5.3: In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself and another, that’s also dependent on contact.
translation_text.sn12.24:5.4: In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact.
translation_text.sn12.24:6.1: Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.
translation_text.sn12.24:6.2: In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.
translation_text.sn12.24:6.3: In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself and another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.
translation_text.sn12.24:6.4: In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.”
translation_text.sn12.24:7.1: Venerable Ānanda heard this discussion between Venerable Sāriputta and those wanderers of other religions.
translation_text.sn12.24:7.2: Then Ānanda wandered for alms in Rājagaha. After the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side,
translation_text.sn12.24:7.3: and informed the Buddha of all they had discussed.
translation_text.sn12.24:8.1: “Good, good, Ānanda! It’s just as Sāriputta has so rightly explained.
translation_text.sn12.24:8.2: I have said that suffering is dependently originated.
translation_text.sn12.24:8.5: Saying this you would repeat what I have said, and not misrepresent me with an untruth. You would explain in line with my teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.
translation_text.sn12.24:9.1: Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. …
translation_text.sn12.24:9.4: In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact.
translation_text.sn12.24:10.1: In the case of those who declare that suffering is made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. …
translation_text.sn12.24:10.4: In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.
translation_text.sn12.24:11.1: Ānanda, this one time I was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
translation_text.sn12.24:11.2: Then I robed up in the morning and, taking my bowl and robe, entered Rājagaha for alms.
translation_text.sn12.24:11.4: ‘It’s too early to wander for alms in Rājagaha.
translation_text.sn12.24:11.5: Why don’t I visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?’
translation_text.sn12.24:12.1: Then I went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there.
translation_text.sn12.24:12.2: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, I sat down to one side. …”
translation_text.sn12.24:13.1: (The wanderers asked the Buddha the very same questions, and he gave the same answers.)
translation_text.sn12.24:17.2: how the whole matter is stated with one phrase.
translation_text.sn12.24:17.3: Could there be a detailed explanation of this matter that is both deep and appears deep?”
translation_text.sn12.24:18.1: “Well then, Ānanda, clarify this matter yourself.”
translation_text.sn12.24:18.2: “Sir, suppose they were to ask me:
translation_text.sn12.24:18.3: ‘Reverend Ānanda, what is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of old age and death?’
translation_text.sn12.24:18.5: ‘Reverends, rebirth is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of old age and death.’
translation_text.sn12.24:18.6: That’s how I’d answer such a question.
translation_text.sn12.24:19.1: Suppose they were to ask me:
translation_text.sn12.24:19.4: ‘Continued existence is the source of rebirth.’
translation_text.sn12.24:19.5: That’s how I’d answer such a question.
translation_text.sn12.24:20.1: Suppose they were to ask me:
translation_text.sn12.24:20.2: ‘What is the source of continued existence?’
translation_text.sn12.24:20.4: ‘Grasping is the source of continued existence.’
translation_text.sn12.24:20.5: That’s how I’d answer such a question.
translation_text.sn12.24:21.1: Suppose they were to ask me:
translation_text.sn12.24:21.5: Suppose they were to ask me:
translation_text.sn12.24:21.8: ‘The six sense fields are the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of contact.’
translation_text.sn12.24:21.9: ‘When the six fields of contact fade away and cease with nothing left over, contact ceases.
translation_text.sn12.24:21.13: When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases.
translation_text.sn12.24:21.14: When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases.
translation_text.sn12.24:21.15: When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease.
translation_text.sn12.24:21.16: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’
translation_text.sn12.24:21.17: That’s how I’d answer such a question.”
